final_answer: 1. Gather time‑series metrics for cache hit/miss, DB read count, CPU usage and request volume per endpoint (N1:0:8bdbd64a). 2. Compare these metrics before and after the feature release to identify which endpoints suffered the biggest hit‑rate drop and DB‑read increase (N1:1:fe23f78d). 3. Inspect how the new query parameters are incorporated into cache keys; map the parameters to keys and verify that key cardinality has not exploded (N10:1:2f649bb3, N6:1:b0545de6). 4. Review cache TTL and eviction policy and ensure the cache size can accommodate the larger key space (N10:3:d8d5edc6). 5. Profile the application code handling the new parameters to locate CPU hotspots in query construction or serialization (N6:3:17025a65). 6. Run EXPLAIN (ANALYZE) on the affected SQL statements to detect missing or suboptimal indexes and confirm that the planner still chooses efficient plans (N1:5:c0221df6, N2:4:25d92340). 7. If index or plan issues are found, create or tune multicolumn/covering indexes to match the new WHERE clauses (N2:4:25d92340). 8. As a short‑term mitigation, add back‑off or rate‑limiting on hot paths to reduce DB pressure while refactoring (N1:8:f575b514). 9. After each change, re‑measure the same metrics and repeat the cycle until cache hit rate and DB reads return to acceptable levels (N2:9:bab0286f).
